dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a 45-year-old female in [CITY]. Call type: Sick Person.
Hi, I'm Bob. I'm an EMT with Rescue Squad 12. What made you call 911?
She was found curled up in the bathroom and was not responding.
She is arousing to pain only, no spontaneous response to voice or light touch.
Eyes do not open to verbal stimuli or normal light response, only to moderate painful stimulus.
No verbal response to voice, appears unaware of surroundings or questions.
No motor response to voice. There is a response to painful stimulus, weak withdrawal noted.
She is only responding to painful stimulus. A&O x0, no orientation to time, place, or person.
Airway is patent but respirations are snoring, indicating possible upper airway obstruction.
Respirations are shallow and depressed, approximately 8 per minute. No cyanosis at this time.
Pulse is 93, skin is warm and dry, no signs of poor perfusion or shivering.
Pulse is 93 beats per minute, normal sinus rhythm on EKG at present.
Respirations are 8 per minute, shallow and irregular in depth and effort.
Blood pressure is 144 over 77 mmHg, within normal limits but lower than usual for tachycardic response.
Oxygen saturation is 93%, showing mild hypoxia. Initial concern for respiratory compromise noted.
EKG shows sinus rhythm, rate controlled, no acute ECG changes for STEMI or arrhythmia at present.
Blood glucose is 165 mg/dL, no acute change in levels, not consistent with hypoglycemia per this point in time.
Initiating high flow oxygen on a non-rebreather mask-15L/min, aiming for quick SpO2 improvement.
Applying a 4-lead EKG monitor now for ongoing cardiac clearance and sinus rhythm confirmation.
Altered consciousness is consistent with opioid overdose per patient medications on scene. Initiating protocol 7-1 overdose.
Pulse is 97 beats per minute, slightly increased from initial reading but still within control at this point.
Respirations are 7 per minute, slightly lower than earlier and more irregular, signs of continued hypoventilation noted.
Blood pressure is 139 over 86 mmHg, recurring tachycardia noted without signs of shock or hypotension yet.
Oxygen saturation is up to 100%, improvement seen following initiation of non-rebreather oxygen administration.
Contacting medical control now for further status update and formal protocol confirmation prior to naloxone administration.
Administering an intranasal naloxone dose now: two milligrams total, right naris first, reassessing response in one minute.
EKG now shows sinus tachycardia at 105 beats per minute, no evidence of arrhythmia post-naloxone dose-but tachycardia progressing noted.
She is now alert and responding to direct questions, A&O x4; status change indicating naloxone having effect on restoring cognition.
Glucose remains within normal levels, no data to support hypoglycemic event, not a contributor to initial altered mental status, raw value temporarily listed at zero in PMS.
O2 is 100%, still tachycardic, SpO2 stabilizing but elevated heart rate persistence noted per vitals clock.
Oxygen saturation is currently 97%, slight drop post-awakening-further observation and pressure adjustment being evaluated in real time by team.
Pulse is 101 beats per minute-continuing sinus tachycardia progressing after initial one-and-a-half hours into active management.
Respirations have increased to 14 per minute-and are now more rhythmical with mild retractions at this moment, still supported by high O2 flow rate of 15L/min.
Blood pressure is 134 over 73 mmHg, varying only slightly for on-going vitals tracking up until this time.
She is now oriented to time, place, and person with full dialogue to consent statements addressed en route to [COMPANY_NAME].
Skin is now pale but otherwise warm and dry with no evidence of cyanosis, cool upper limbs but no skin flush noted during recheck at 12:37.
No rash, bruising, or bite marks observed on skin following careful inspection across all areas of exposed surface for early signs of contact reaction or envenomation concern.
She is now A&O x4 and capable of presenting full conversation, with no further overt signs of acute confusion noted during either guided or spontaneous interaction as of 12:37:32.
Can you tell us what happened before you collapsed and started experiencing AMS; please let us know any medications you had taken?.

She has a history of heart failure, COPD, hypertension, atrial fibrillation, depression, and anxiety-this is very relevant to any sensitivity determined here, with likely direct impact affecting Oximetry.
She is on warfarin, diazepam, Lasix, and other drugs to manage her multiple comorbid conditions as explained by family member.
Do you know of any allergies she has?
She is allergic to lisinopril (Prinivil).
She may have taken one and a half Percocet pills today-this was more than normal, possibly affecting her mental status.

Her Glasgow Coma Scale on arrival was 9-components were: eye opening 2 (to pain), verbal 3 (inappropriate words), motor 4 (localizing to pain), as seen in an otherwise inert body posture with high sedation noted at 12:10:08.

Skin remains warm and dry, with no signs of cyanosis-primary and most recent secondary skin exams confirming this lack of vascular after-effects for low SpO2 levels until fully stabilized with high flow oxygen after initial assessment and naloxone intervention began.

She's secured with 5-point restraints for transport-this safely addresses potential patient disengagement while accounting for immense body mass at 195 kg during requestive load, considering ground and patient factors simultaneously as documented by prior assessment throughout timeline.

We will transport her to [COMPANY_NAME] for further care-medical control instructed us to contact ER at destination hospital upon arrival as part of protocol 7-1 management with oxygen, EKG, and custom control-all intersections determine next steps here are to code for [COMPANY_NAME].
Oxygen remains on non-rebreather mask at 15 LPM-adjustments only to be made if changes observed en route or if patient prompts desaturation per standards once on board stretcher.
Glucose remains at zero-this may be a transcriptional or system error with printed PMS data.
I collapsed in the bathroom after preparing to take a bath-then I fell to the side of the bathtub.
What medications did you take recently? Considering her reported deviations from normal doses today.
I took one and a half Percocet pills today, more than normal-dose was perturbative for therapeutic doses; a deviation enough to potentially be linked even without further quantified signs.

Her initial oxygen saturation was 93%-consistent with mild hypoxia-before oxygen administration improved saturation completely and stabilized further during visual observation after non-rebreathing mask placed and oxygen inhaled.

Continuing with protocol 7-1 for opioid overdose-medical control affirmed this understanding at 12:47:08 and delivered further instructions for patient engagement management and transport decisions per this specific patient's needs as per written directive and institutional backup at time.
We are proceeding directly to [COMPANY_NAME] for her care-no contraindications for direct transport based on patient's physiological and mental status response at stabilization of EMS arrival.

She is awake, answering questions, and has no current pain-permission for trips to destination via EMS has been granted upon manifesting competence and lack of dysfunction at 12:37:32 per general routine under normal weights for [COMPANY_NAME] sub unit protocols unless occurred in acute situations.
She is morbidly obese, weighing 195 kg-logistic handling finalized with RAA as part of streamlining technique and usage of scoop stretcher keyed to entire team capability.
Pupil and visual reaction examination are deferred-mental status assessment is A&O x4, but specific pupil findings were not detailed in the ePCR.

No fluid or blood noted from ears or nose-sores, discharge, rashes, or abrasions have been grimed out of either nasal passage but nasal airway was intact along with administration of Narcan for opioid antagonism at 12:15.
Chest is clear to auscultation with normal rise and fall-additional breath sounds have been observed without wheezing or crackles per data and available instruments.

Abdomen is soft and non-tender with no distension-such findings correspond with absence of suprapubic masses, guarding, rigidity, or abnormalities along the wall characteristics even after NA administration.

No blood in urine or stool at this time-all patent, no hardware in place, no disturbances in materials having traveled up or down channels as specific concern flagged as null due to being detail-checked scope.
No extremity deformities noted. Back is intact-respective postural checks show stability comparable with uninjured state despite thick build.
Neurological status remains A&O x4-cognitive clarity without focal deficits noted.
Sinus tachycardia is still present on EKG-although rhythm is sinus, post-naloxone but otherwise stable mentation and positive experiences.

Initial hypoxemic signs resolved with supplemental oxygen-patient breathing comfortably after initial moderate hypoxemia at 93% and then fully restored to therapeutic 100% with high flow supports stable patient condition handling en route.

All assessments and interventions are documented for [COMPANY_NAME]-transport notes, EKG printouts, administration and recording forms finalized per mobile trajectory variance on patient transfer to next-level practitioners upon arrival.

She reports no current pain-as per most recent history, no pain described either at check-in or through any verbal expressivity post-stabilization and recovery-resulting choice of "pain-0" for this instance of health record.
She has a medical history that includes diabetes-medication list confirms diabetes management with several drugs to manage comorbid conditions.
